title: Welcome to Blocks 🧱
description: Understand what a Block actually is, and the magical yield keyword.

slides.title: What is a Block? 🧱
slides.content:
You have actually been using Blocks this whole time!

A Block is simply a nameless chunk of code that you can pass directly into a method.

```ruby
# The block is everything between do and end
5.times do
  puts "I am inside a block!"
end
```

slides.title: Blocks vs Methods
slides.content:
Methods can be called over and over again from anywhere because they are saved to your computer's memory (`def name...end`).

Blocks, however, are "anonymous" (nameless). They are one-hit wonders. They only exist attached to a method call, and vanish instantly after they run.

quizData.question: What happens to a Block after it finishes executing?

options.text: It is saved to memory automatically.
It vanishes and cannot be called again, because it has no name.

quizData.explanation: Because a block has no name (it is 'anonymous'), there is no way for the program to grab it and run it a second time.

slides.title: The Yield Keyword 🫴
slides.content:
So, how does the `.times` method actually know to execute the chunk of code you attached to it?

It uses a magical Ruby keyword called **yield**.

When a method hits the word `yield`, it pauses itself and immediately executes whatever block was attached to it by the user.

slides.title: Yielding to a Block
slides.content: Run this! Watch how the method stops executing its own code, "yields" control to the attached block, and then resumes.
slides.codeSnippet:
def my_method
  puts "1. Method starting..."
  yield
  puts "3. Method finished!"
end

# We attach a block to our method
my_method do
  puts "2. I am the Block code!"
end

quizData.question: What does the `yield` keyword do exactly?

options.text: It pauses the current method and runs the code block attached to it.
It permanently breaks out of the method.
It returns a true or false value.

quizData.explanation: To 'yield' means to give way. The method politely yields control to the block, allowing the block to execute before the method finishes its own instructions.
